title: The Third Sunday of Advent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## December 13, 2020 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon falls on the Third Sunday of Advent in the Christian calendar, traditionally known as Gaudete Sunday (from the Latin "rejoice"), characterized by a shift toward joyful anticipation rather than penitential preparation. Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would have included Isaiah 61:1-4, 8-11 (messianic prophecy), 1 Thessalonians 5:16-24 (Paul on rejoicing), and John 1:6-8, 19-28 (John the Baptist's testimony). The liturgical color remains violet/purple, though some churches introduce rose candles on this Sunday to signal the approaching Christmas season just twelve days away.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the midst of significant pandemic-related disruptions to its typical calendar and governance cycles; the 79th General Convention had been postponed from 2021 to 2022 due to COVID-19. The Diocese of Colorado, led by Bishop Robert J. Wright, was navigating reopening decisions for worship and managing the economic strain on parishes from reduced giving and suspended activities. Saint John's Cathedral, as the seat of the diocesan bishop, would have been particularly visible in denominational communications around pandemic protocols and diocesan leadership during this period of uncertainty.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing a winter surge in COVID-19 cases, with hospitalizations rising sharply in mid-December ahead of the holiday season. The Colorado General Assembly was not in session (the legislative session begins in January), but local political focus remained on pandemic response, vaccine distribution logistics, and economic recovery from the spring/summer shutdowns. The Capitol Hill neighborhood where the Cathedral is located had seen ongoing homelessness and housing instability exacerbated by pandemic conditions, with community organizations working overtime on cold-weather shelter capacity.

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The United States was in the grip of a severe winter COVID-19 surge with daily cases exceeding 200,000 nationally; the first vaccines (Pfizer-BioNTech) had just received emergency use authorization on December 11, two days before this sermon, creating both hope and logistical chaos around rollout. The Trump administration was in its final weeks following the November election, with ongoing legal challenges to results in various states and significant political polarization. Economic hardship was deepening as pandemic-related unemployment persisted and Congress remained deadlocked over additional stimulus relief, with many Americans facing eviction moratoriums set to expire.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The United Kingdom and European Union were racing toward the January 1 Brexit deadline with significant trade deal negotiations still unresolved, creating economic uncertainty across the Atlantic. Iran was dealing with political fallout and international tension following the assassination of nuclear scientist Mohsen Fakhrizadeh on November 27, blamed on Israel, which had elevated regional security concerns. Australia and other Pacific nations were beginning vaccination planning while maintaining strict border controls, having controlled community transmission throughout 2020.

CULTURAL MOMENT

America in mid-December 2020 was marked by profound ambivalence: grief and fatigue from nine months of pandemic, cautious optimism about vaccines, and deep political division following the contested election. The Christmas season carried unusual weight—family gatherings faced health risks, many traditional holiday experiences were cancelled or virtual, and there was a hunger for spiritual meaning and community that was simultaneously impossible to fully satisfy. The mood at Saint John's Cathedral on this Sunday would have blended Advent's traditional themes of waiting and hope with the acute present-moment reality of a nation approaching 300,000 COVID deaths, making Gaudete Sunday's call to "rejoice" particularly poignant and theologically complex.
